test.name: Historical legal terminology in an 1832 will
test.description: Tests whether the skill correctly explains archaic legal terms commonly found in 18th and 19th century English and American wills — specifically 'relict' (surviving spouse) and 'appurtenances' (attached property/rights). Tests terminology lookup and research-actionable guidance.

input.user_message: An 1832 will for my ancestor uses the words 'relict' and 'appurtenances.' What do these mean and how do they help my research?

judge_context: Must correctly define 'relict' as the surviving spouse — widow or widower — not a relic or leftover item
Must correctly define 'appurtenances' as property or rights attached to the main property, such as outbuildings, land rights, or easements
Must explain that identifying the relict in a will names the surviving spouse, which is a key genealogical fact — it gives the spouse's name and confirms the marital relationship
Should note that these are standard legal terms found in 18th and 19th century English and American probate documents
Should give research-actionable guidance: look for the relict in subsequent census records, land records, or remarriage records to continue tracing the family
